coredata.dc:title: Synergistic degradation of tetracycline by CDs decorated g-C3N4 under LED light irradiation combined with the persulfate-based advanced oxidation process
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chao
Liu, Yali
Kang, Xiaorong
Li, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN) has been applied to degrade a variety of pollutants. However, two major defects, the low utilization of light energy and the high recombination of photogenerated electron and hole (e--h+) pairs, need to be improved. In this study, a LED-light-driven photocatalyst, carbon dots decorated graphitic carbon nitride (CNC), was successfully prepared by simple thermal polymerization. Due to the remarkable up-conversion photoluminescence properties and superior charge transport capability of CDs, CNC had higher e--h+ separation efficiency and broader visible light response. In this experiment, a combined process of CNC and persulfate (CNC/PS) was used for tetracycline (TC) degradation. High-efficient photocatalytic degradation rate of 81.9% was obtained in the CNC/PS system. Moreover, the mechanism analysis indicated that the reaction between PS and photogenerated e- effectively promoted the separation of electrons-holes pairs, thereby enhancing the formation of dominant active radicals •O2 - and holes for TC degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Persulfate
Carbon dots
Tetracycline
LED light

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of catalysts on TC degradation
Key operating parameters of CNC/PS system
Roles of CNC/PS in the TC degradation
Role of active substances in photocatalytic degradation of TC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of CN and CNC.
SEM images of CN and CNC (a and b); HRTEM image of CDs (c); SEM-EDS elemental mapping for CNC (d-f).
XPS spectra of CN and CNC (a); High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) regions.
UV–vis DRS (a), and the band gap determined from the (αhv)1/2 versus photon-energy (b) of CN and CNC.
VB-XPS (a) and schematic band structure evolution (b) of CN and CNC.
PL spectra of CN and CNC excited at a wavelength of 350 nm (a), and Up-converted photoluminescence spectra of CDs (b).
TC degradation curves (a) and kobs (b) in different photocatalytic systems (photocatalyst dose: 0.25 g/L, initial TC concentration: 10 mg/L, PS dose: 2 mM, pH: 7).
Degradation efficiencies and kobs of TC in the CNC/PS system at different CNC doses (a), PS doses (b), initial pH values (c), and initial TC concentrations (d).
Effect of anions on TC degradation (a), and the cyclic experiment (b) in CNC/PS system.
Effect of different scavengers on the TC degradation in the CNC/PS system.
ESR spectra of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•SO4 - (a), DMPO-•O2 - (b) in the CNC/PS system under LED light or in the dark.
Transient photocurrent responses (a), and EIS Nyquist plot (b) of CN and CNC electrodes.
LED light-driven photocatalytic mechanism of CNC/PS for TC degradation.
Possible degradation pathway of TC for CNC/PS system.

head.ce:title: Synergistic degradation of tetracycline by CDs decorated g-C3N4 under LED light irradiation combined with the persulfate-based advanced oxidation process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Engineering, Nanjing Institute of Technology, Nanjing 211167, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Engineering, Nanjing Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Engineering, Nanjing Institute of Technology, Nanjing 211167, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN) has been applied to degrade a variety of pollutants. However, two major defects, the low utilization of light energy and the high recombination of photogenerated electron and hole (e--h+) pairs, need to be improved. In this study, a LED-light-driven photocatalyst, carbon dots decorated graphitic carbon nitride (CNC), was successfully prepared by simple thermal polymerization. Due to the remarkable up-conversion photoluminescence properties and superior charge transport capability of CDs, CNC had higher e--h+ separation efficiency and broader visible light response. In this experiment, a combined process of CNC and persulfate (CNC/PS) was used for tetracycline (TC) degradation. High-efficient photocatalytic degradation rate of 81.9% was obtained in the CNC/PS system. Moreover, the mechanism analysis indicated that the reaction between PS and photogenerated e- effectively promoted the separation of electrons-holes pairs, thereby enhancing the formation of dominant active radicals •O2 - and holes for TC degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, tetracycline (TC) as a broad-spectrum antibiotic has been used to prevent and treat bacterial infections [1]. Due to its stable chemical structure and non-biodegradable characteristics, most TC molecules are not degraded by organisms, and are released into the aquatic environment and continuously accumulated, posing a serious threat to the ecology and human health [2–4]. Therefore, it is particularly necessary to seek a productive and cost-effective method of removing TC before it is discharged into the water bodies.

The photocatalytic reaction is regarded as a promising technology to solve environmental governances [5–7]. Some traditional photocatalysts, such as TiO2, have shown their potential in degrading TC under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation [8,9]. UV light excited electrons (e-) from the valence band (VB) of TiO2 to its conduction band (CB), and the holes (h+) left in VB directly oxidized the adsorbed TC [8–10]. However, the practical application of these photocatalysts is hindered due to their poor utilization of visible light energy [8]. In fact, photocatalytic performances largely depend on the adsorption capacity of semiconductors to light and the reaction efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers [11]. Therefore, tremendous efforts have been made to prepare visible-light-sensitive semiconductors by band structure engineering, such as multi-metal oxides, sulfides, and oxynitrides [12,13]. Especially from the perspective of sustainable development, it is reasonably necessary to prepare non-metallic, non-toxic, non-polluting visible light semiconductors.

Recently, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN), an innovative visible-light-driven photocatalyst, has gotten extensive attention due to its advantages of nontoxicity, excellent stability, easy fabrication, and low cost [14,15]. As we all know, CN has a narrow bandgap of 2.7 eV and thus leads to a suitable visible-light response, making it easy to generate electron-hole (e--h+) pairs [16]. Nevertheless, the two primary defects of CN, the easy recombination of photogenerated e--h+ pairs and the low visible spectrum, need to be resolved urgently. In order to ameliorate these shortcomings, a variety of methods have been adopted to modify and optimize CN，including non-metal doping [17], metal deposition [18], and coupling with other materials to form hybrids [19].

As previously reported, a potential strategy to improve the photocatalytic performances of CN is to effectively capture photogenerated e- in CN using carbon nanomaterials (e.g., fullerene, carbon nanotube, and graphene), thereby preventing charges recombination [20]. The recently developed carbon dots (CDs) with a size of less than 10 nm are mainly composed of graphitic carbon (sp2 carbon), which has been used to spectral light response because of their remarkable up-conversion photoluminescence property and superior charge transmission ability [21]. The broad-band light absorption characteristics of CDs conferred by quantum effects make it used as a light absorber and coupled with semiconductor nanoparticles (such as BiVO4) to effectively improve its photocatalytic performance [22]. Furthermore, CDs are also capable of acting as electron acceptors for the flow of photogenerated charge carriers [23]. Based on the similar π -conjugated structure of CN and CDs, doping CDs into CN (CNC) to give full play to their respective advantages has been proved to be a photocatalytic system with practical application prospects [24,25].

In addition to the rational design of the photocatalyst structure, the combination of photocatalysis and the advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) is considered another way to enhance catalytic performance [26]. Recent studies have found that AOPs based on persulfate (PS) has the advantages of superior stability, good water solubility, universal pH (3−9), longevity of existence (30–40 µs), and high redox potential (sulfate radicals (•SO4 -, 2.5–3.1 eV)) [26–28]. Additionally, PS can be activated by UV light, ultrasound, alkali, electricity, transition metal ions, and biological activated carbon, thereby further improving the ability of various organic pollutants to be degraded into biodegradable or non-hazardous products [29]. Consequently, the combination of photocatalysis and PS-based AOPs is an effective method for organic pollutants degradation. That can be explained from the following two aspects: For one thing, the emerging photocatalyst acts as a new activator, enabling PS to be more effectively activated and enhancing the oxidation of organic pollutants [18]. For another, PS is used as an electron trap to prevent the photogenerated e--h+ pairs from recombining [30]. However, the performances and mechanisms of the combined process of CNC and PS (CNC/PS) on the degradation of TC are still unclear.

Therefore, the main purpose of this work is to construct a LED light-driven photocatalyst CNC, and introduce PS into CNC (CNC/PS) to study its degradation performance and mechanism for TC. The CNC was prepared using urea and CDs solution, and its physical and chemical properties were characterized. Furthermore, batch tests were conducted to investigate the vital operating parameters (initial pH, TC concentrations, photocatalyst doses, and PS doses) that affect TC degradation, as well as the operational stability of the CNC/PS system. Simultaneously, the TC degradation pathway was analyzed by high- performance liquid chromatography and mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS). In addition, active species quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments were carried out to further identify the major active species participating in TC degradation in the CNC/PS system.

The urea, TC, potassium persulfate, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), citric acid, 4-benzoquinone (BQ), and methyl alcohol (MeOH) used in this experiment were all analytically pure, and they were purchased from Shanghai Mack-Lin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd, Shanghai, China.

The CDs were constructed by the hydrothermal method described in previous literature [31]. Briefly, 3 g of citric acid and 1 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water by ultrasound (frequency 20 Hz, ultrasonic time 2 h). Next, the mixed solution was put into a 100 mL stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon and remained at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling the autoclave to ambient temperature, the black solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min (TDZ4-WS, Xiangyi, China) to obtain CDs liquid. Then, the CDs was stocked at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

CNC was synthesized using the polymerization method [32]. The precursor of CNC used in this study was prepared by adding 100 µL of CDs solution and 10 g of urea into 20 mL of ultrapure water, magnetic stirring for 3 h, and water bathed heating to 85 °C to remove water. Firstly, 10 g of the precursor was put into an alumina crucible with a volume of 30 mL and then wrapped in tin foil. Secondly, the crucible was placed into a muffle furnace (SX2–12–10, Shanghai, China) with a heating rate of 2.5 °C∙min−1, and kept at 550 °C for 1.5 h. Afterward, it was continuously calcined at 500 °C (heating rate: 5 °C∙min−1) for another 1.5 h. After cooling the furnace to ambient temperature, the CNC was obtained by washing with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol three times, respectively, and drying at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalysts on TC degradation
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of photocatalyst (CN or CNC, 25 mg) was added into 100 mL of TC aqueous solution (10 mg∙L−1) and dispersed by an ultrasonic cleaner (KS-600D, China). Next, the pH values were adjusted to 8.0 using 0.1 M HCl and/or NaOH solution, respectively. Then, the mixed liquor was placed in the dark and stirred with a magnetic mixer for 30 min to obtain an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After adding 2 mM K2S2O8 into the mixed liquor, the mixed liquor was irradiated with a 30 W LED lamp (wavelength: 450–750 nm) for 0–60 min 5 mL samples were taken out at regular intervals, filtrated through a cellulose acetate membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm, quenched with MeOH, and finally analyzed with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 357 nm.

Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) simplified pseudo-first order kinetic model was used to describe the photocatalytic degradation rate of TC, as shown in Eq. (1) [32].
(1)
ln
(
c
t
c
0
)
= −
k
obs
t
where kobs (min−1) represents the pseudo-first order rate constant; C0 and Ct (mg∙L−1) are the TC concentrations in the system at the initial and time t (min), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Key operating parameters of CNC/PS system
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the best operating parameters, the effects of CNC doses (0.05–1.0 g/L), PS doses (0–10 mM), initial TC concentration (5–20 mg/L), and initial pH (2−12) on TC degradation were investigated. All tests were performed in 250 mL conical flasks, in each of which 100 mL of TC were placed. The other operating conditions were described in section 2.4.1. At the end of the photocatalytic reaction, the HPLC-MS was used to analyze the intermediate products of TC degradation.

Anion interference experiment and cyclic experiment were carried out to study the stability of CNC/PS robustness of CNC/PS. In this study, the CO3 2-, SO4 2-, Cl- and NO3 - were selected, and the concentration of each anion was determined to be 100 mg/L. Also, Before the next cycle, the CNC used in the previous cycle should be centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, washed with deionized water for twice, and dried at 60 °C for 1 d.

The roles of reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), h+, superoxide radicals (•O2 -), and •SO4 -, were indirectly investigated via scavenger experiments. In this study, TBA, EDTA, MeOH, and VC were selected as scavengers. Except for adding 1 mM scavenger, the detailed experimental procedures were the same as those described in the “Photocatalytic activity tests”. Additionally, the ESR was analyzed on a Bruker model spectrometer (Brukeemxplus, Brooke, German) with 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) (Aladdin Industrial Corporation) as a radical spin trapping agent.
All tests were repeated three times, and the average and standard deviation were recorded.

XRD patterns of CN and CNC were depicted in Fig. 1a. Both CN and CNC exhibited obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ of 13.0° and 27.5°, corresponding to the periodical planer structural stacking of the aromatic system and the interfacial stacking structure of graphite segments, respectively, which pointed to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of CN (JCPDS No.87–1526) [33]. The enhanced intensity of diffraction peak (002) was observed in the CNC, indicating that the introduction of CDs contributed to the layered crystal structure of CN.

The FT-IR diagrams of CN and CNC were shown in Fig. 1b. The band at 800 cm−1 was the respiration of the triazine units, while the wide characteristic vibration modes at 3000–3700 cm−1 corresponded to the hydroxyl species (C-OH) and the free amino groups (N-H) adsorbed on the CN surface [34]. Several bands appearing at 1200–1700 cm−1 represented stretching vibration of C-N heterocycles [35]. The characteristic signals of C-OH, C-N heterocycles, and triazine units did not change with the CDs, revealing that the stacked structures of CN were not deformed. It is important to note that, compared with the band in CN, the band in the CNC had slight shifts from 1247 to 1239 cm−1, and from 1415 to 1406 cm−1 (shown in Fig. S1). This result suggested that CDs was loaded well to CN, and their strong interactions weakened the bond strength of C-N [36].

The morphologies and microstructures of CN and CNC were characterized via SEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 2, CN had a typical layered structure and irregular pores, which should be attributed to the gas released by the decomposition of urea during the calcination process [37]. The CNC showed a layered and smooth morphology (in Fig.S2b), which was consistent with the previous study [38]. Furthermore, the basic sheet of CN and CNC was curved and its edges were ragged, which might be due to the thinner material obtained by the second calcination.

Different from the typical sheet-like morphology of CN (Fig. S2), many nanosheets with laminar morphology, such as silk veil, were observed in the CNC. This indicated that the nanoplate aggregates could be dispersed. As described in Fig. 2c, most the CDs were evenly decorated on the surface of CNC. Meanwhile, it is also found that some CDs still aggregated into larger particles with a diameter of 5–10 nm due to thermal polymerization, and adhered to the CN surface through π-π interactions [36]. Fluorescence tests indicated that CDs exhibited a bright blue emission under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Fig. S2). It might be because the urea used in the preparation made amino groups doping on the surface of CDs to reduce non-radiative recombination centers [39].

From the XPS measurement spectra of CN and CNC ( Fig. 3a), it can be seen that only three elements of C1s, N1s, and O1s were detected. The N/C in CNC was 47.50%, which was 6.16% higher than that in the CN. This was because the CDs prepared in this experiment were doped with N from urea. The O1s peak was caused by oxygen-containing substances adsorbed on the surface of the precursor during the calcination process in an air environment.

For CN (Fig. 3b), the XPS spectra of C1s at 284.85 and 288.16 eV were assigned to CC bonds and sp2 carbon atoms bonded to N in an aromatic ring (N-CN), respectively [40]. There was no change of these two peaks in the CNC, suggesting that the doping CDs into CN could not affect the structure of triazine rings. This result was consistent with the XRD analysis. At the same time, a new peak of 285.47 eV corresponding to C-OH was observed in the CNC, which proved that the hydroxyl-rich CDs were assembled on CN [41]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 398.2, 399.4, 400.7, and 404.4 eV, respectively, corresponding to sp2-bonded N in the triazine rings (C-NC), tertiary N in N-(C)3, free amino groups (N-H) and graphitic species. Similar to the spectrum of C 1 s, the O 1 s of CN had two peaks at 531.4 and 533.2 eV (Fig. 3d), assigning to C-O and OH/C-O-C, respectively. Meanwhile, a new peak appeared at 534.6 eV in the CNC, corresponding to the OC-O, which can be explained by the significant increase in the number of resident carboxyl groups due to the introduction of CDs [42]. The above results indicated that the π–π stacking interaction between CN and CDs was the main reason for CNC synthesis [36].

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of both CN and CNC (Fig. S3) exhibited type IV curves with a significant hysteresis return line in the range of 0.6–1.0 P/P0, reflecting the mesoporous nature of the materials. The BET specific surface area of CNC was 13.65 m2/g, which is about 2.2 times higher than that of CN (6.19 m2/g), probably due to the secondary calcination.

As we know, the light-harvesting ability of photocatalyst is essential to its photocatalytic activity. To investigate the optical absorption properties of CN and CNC, the UV–vis DRS was examined. In Fig. 4a, the intrinsic absorption edge of CN and CNC were found at 461 and 517 nm, respectively. Compared with CN catalyst, CNC showed an obvious redshift, indicating a stronger visible light response. The results demonstrated that CDs could effectively increase the visible light absorption capacity of CNC.

Simultaneously, in order to further study the optical properties of CN and CNC, the optical band gaps were obtained according to the Tauc plot function:
(2)
( α h v )
2 / n
= A ( h v −
E
g
)
Where α, h, v, A, and Eg represent adsorption coefficient, Planck constant, optical frequency, proportional coefficient, and bandgap. In this study, the CN and CNC samples were all indirect transitions, and the n value related to the type of optical transition were 4.

The bandgap map was drawn with (αhv)1/2 as ordinate and hv as abscissa. It can be derived from Fig. 4b, the calculated bandgaps of CN and CNC were 2.73 and 2.41 eV, respectively. The bandgap of CN was relatively wider. When it is recombined with CDs, the gap width changed and became narrow, which facilitated the transition of photogenerated carrier from the VB to the CB, and increased the generation rate of e--h+ pair.

The band structure of CN as well as CNC were further characterized by VB-XPS. As described in Fig. 5a, the VB values of the CN and CNC were estimated to be 1.38 and 1.47 eV, respectively. Taking into account the error range of the contact potential between the test appliance and photocatalyst [37], the final VB values of CN and CNC relative to a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) were determined according to the following formula, Eq. (3):
(3)
E
V L
= 4.44 +
E
N H E
− ϕ
Where EVL, ENHE, and Φ represented potential of vacuum level, the potential of NHE, and the work function of the instrument (4.29 eV), respectively.

The VB values of CN and CNC were 1.53 and 1.62 eV, respectively, based on the calculation. Combined with the bandgap in Fig. 3b, the CB values of CN and CNC could be deduced to − 1.20 and − 0.79 eV according to above Eq. (3), respectively, as illustrated in the energy band structure diagrams of Fig. 5b.

PL analysis is often used to study the separation efficiency of photogenerated e--h+ pairs. It is well known that lower fluorescence emission intensity indicates a lower e--h+ recombination rate and higher photocatalytic activity [43]. In this study, the PL emission spectrum of CN had a strong emission peak at 450 nm, while the PL intensity of CNC was much lower than that of CN ( Fig. 6a). This result suggested that the introduction of CDs promoted the separation of e--h+, which could be attributed to the influential role played by N atoms in the conjugated C plane [44].

In order to analyze the reasons for the good optical performance of CNC, the superior up-converted photoluminescence property of CDs was investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 6b when the CDs were excited by long-wavelength light of 650–850 nm, the up-conversion emissions were significant in the range of 375–480 nm. Similar to the conclusions of previous studies [31], this result also showed that CNC doped with CDs could convert near-infrared emission wavelengths into UV light, thereby improving photocatalytic efficiency.

As mentioned above, the introduction of CDs not only enhanced the visible light absorption performance of CNC but also improved the separation of e--h+ pairs, resulting in a significantly higher LED photocatalytic activity of CNC than CN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Roles of CNC/PS in the TC degradation

ce:section.ce:para: Effects of CNC and/or PS on TC degradation were shown in Fig. 7. As shown in Fig. 7a, the TC concentrations in the blank test remained relatively stable due to their non-degradable nature. Simultaneously, there was no obvious change in the TC removal with PS addition alone, no matter it was in the dark or light on. It meant that the oxidation effect of PS itself on TC was extremely weak, and it could not be activated by the LED light. In the 30-minute dark experiments, the TC removal rates of CN and CNC were 3% and 7%, respectively, and after 60 min of LED illumination, the TC removal rates reached 29.6% and 63.9%. This implied that the CDs significantly enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CNC. Surprisingly, the TC removal rates of CN/PS and CNC/PS were increased to 41% and 81.9% respectively. This result demonstrated that PS played a significant role in the photocatalytic degradation process of TC, and the mechanisms will be investigated deeply in the Section 3.4.
From the Fig. 7b, the change rule of kobs was consistent with the TC removal rate, following the order: CNC/PS (0.0299) > CNC (0.0124) > CN/PS (0.0041) > CN (0.0023) > PS (0.0004) > Blank (0.0002).

To explore the performance of CNC/PS system in a complex environment, the influence of different environmental factors on the TC degradation were investigated (CNC doses, PS doses, initial pH values, and TC concentrations). The degradation efficiencies of TC at different CNC doses were presented in Fig. 8a. When the doses of CNC increased from 0.05 to 0.25 g/L, the removal rate of TC increased from 60.4% to 81.9%, and the corresponding kobs increased from 0.0184 to 0.0299. This might be attributed to the fact that more e- and h+ provided by the larger dose of CNC, which was more conducive to activating PS to produce •SO4 -. However, the TC degradation rate decreased slightly when the CNC doses exceeded 0.25 g/L, possibly because the light transmittance of the solution decreased with the increase of CNC. Therefore, the optimum amount of CNC was determined to be 0.25 g/L in the following tests.

The effect of PS doses on TC degradation was shown in Fig. 8b. The removal rate of TC first increased and then decreased with the increase of PS doses. The turning point occurred at the PS dose of 2.0 mM, and the corresponding removal rate and kobs after a reaction time of 60 min was 81.9% and 0.0299, respectively. This might be due to limited number of photogenerated e- produced by a small amount of CNC, which could not fully activate the PS [45]. The reduction of TC degradation rate at high PS doses was mainly due to the reaction of excessive S2O8 2- with •SO4 -, which was similar to the quenching effect of excessive H2O2 on •OH in the Fenton system [46]. Therefore, 2.0 mM PS was selected for better removal effect and lower activator consumption.

The effect of initial TC concentrations on degradation trend in the CNC/PS system was shown in Fig. 8c. As the initial TC concentration increased from 5 to 20 mg/L, the removal rate of TC decreased from 86.1% to 64.8%; corresponding kobs decreased from 0.0413 to 0.0175. The negative correlation between TC degradation ability and initial concentration was mainly limited by the fixed number of catalysts.

As displayed in Fig. 8d, the pH values of the solution, in the range of 2.0–12.0, had almost no effect on the TC removal rate and kobs in the CNC/PS system. There are following two reasons to explain this phenomenon. When the pH value was neutral or even lower, the positive charges on the surface of CNC increased, which helped attract and make S2O8 2- get e- to form a stronger oxidant •SO4 - for accelerating TC degradation [11]. Under alkaline conditions, some •SO4 - reacted with OH- to generate •OH with shorter lifetime, weaker redox potential and lower selectivity [47]. Therefore, a high concentration of OH- might quench •SO4 - and reduce the removal efficiency of TC.

Effects of CO3 2-, SO4 2-, Cl- and NO3 - on the degradation of TC are displayed in Fig. 9a. It can be observed that SO4 2-, Cl- and NO3 - slightly accelerated the photocatalytic activity, while the introduction of CO3 2- significantly promoted the TC degradation in the first 30 min, and then remained stable. The CO3 2- reacted with •SO4 - to generate •CO3 - with higher stability, thereby improving the oxidation performance of CNC/PS to TC. Nevertheless, CO3 2- itself not only acted as a blocking agent for •OH but also competed with TC for h+, weakening the degradable ability to TC in the later stage.

The photocatalytic stability of CNC is very important for practical applications. Thus, the stability of CNC/PS was tested by measuring the photodegradation of TC in a five-cycle experiment. As illustrated in Fig. 9b, the removal efficiency of TC maintained around 80% even in the 5th cycle. Therefore, CNC had excellent photostability and reusability, which was of great value to practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of active substances in photocatalytic degradation of TC
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature, the TBA, EDTA, and BQ were the scavengers for •OH, h+, and •O2 - [30], respectively, and the MeOH could remove •SO4 - and •OH simultaneously [48]. In the CNC/PS reaction system, the photocatalytic degradation rates of TC with different scavengers addition are shown in Fig. 10. It is found that the TC degradation rates after adding TBA, MeOH, EDTA, and BQ were 72.1%, 67.4%, 33.3%, and 18.1%, respectively, which were 10.2%, 14.9%, 49.0%, and 67.6% lower than that in the blank experiment (82.3%). Furthermore, based on the data of TBA and MeOH, it could be concluded that the contribution of •SO4 - to the TC degradation rate was only 4.7%. These results indicated that in the CNC/PS system, the effect of each active species on TC degradation was as follows: •O2 - > h+ > •OH > •SO4 -. Therefore, h+ and •O2 - were the main reactive species in this photocatalytic system. Therefore, the TC degradation in the CNC/PS relied on the complex interactions between multiple active substances, rather than their simple superposition.

In order to verify the existence of •OH, •SO4 -, and •O2 - in the TC degradation process, ESR experiments were performed, and the results are shown in Fig. 11. After 10 min of LED irradiation, the signals of DMPO-•OH were observed in Fig. 11a, and the characteristic quadruplet was 1:2:2:1. It was more than sufficient to prove that the •OH was already present at 10 min of degradation. In addition, the DMPO-•SO4 - signals after PS adding suggested that •SO4 - was formed in the CNC/PS system. In Fig. 11b, the characteristic of DMPO-•O2 - adduct, a four-line ESR signal with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1, was distinctly detected. It was worth noting that no ESR signal was found in the dark environment because it was quite difficult for CNC to activate PS in this case.

Apart from the above reactive species generation analysis, in this study, by measuring the transient photocurrent response of CN and CNC, the charge generation and recombination behavior were investigated. Under the lamp irradiation, record the reproducible photocurrent responses during five “on-off” intermittent irradiation cycles (in Fig. 12a) confirmed the excellent stability of CN and CNC photoelectrodes. It is worth noting from the intensity-time (I-t) curves that, compared with CN electrodes, the introduction of CDs led to an approximately two-fold increase in photocurrent density of CNC. The photocurrent was generated from the transmission of photogenerated e- and the simultaneous capture of photogenerated h+ by scavengers, so the enhanced photocurrent of CNC demonstrated that CDs was beneficial to the effective generation of photogenerated e- and the separation of e--h+ pairs.

To verify the charges transfer behavior of CNC, the EIS Nyquist curves of CN and CNC were compared in Fig. 12b (dark environment and under light conditions). Compared with the CN and CNC images obtained in the dark, the radius of the two electrodes under irradiation was much smaller, which was consistent with the interfacial transfer efficiency of the catalysts. Moreover, in the Nyquist plot, the semicircle of CNC exhibited a smaller radius than that of CN. As previously reported, the radius of the semicircle in the EIS Nyquist diagram was closely related to the charge transfer at the photoelectrode interface, that is, the smaller the radius, the more effective the charge transfer process was [49]. Therefore, the results in this experiment implied that the charge transfer process at the heterojunction interface of CNC was faster than that of the CN, and thus promoting effective separation of the photogenerated e--h+ pairs.

Based on the above experimental results, the test of the electrochemical workstation, and scientific literature [50,51], the cooperation mechanism for CNC/PS system was proposed in Fig. 13. Under LED irradiation, CNC directly absorbed light with a wavelength of < 517 nm to form e--h+ pairs, simultaneously, could convert the light of wavelength > 650 nm to be < 480 nm by incorporating CDs, and then generate e--h+ pairs. Notably, the π–π stacking interaction and relative band alignment of CN and CDs enhanced the migration of photogenerated e- from CN to CDs. Therefore, the CNC not only expanded the light absorption region to visible light by loading CDs to promote the generation of e--h+ pairs, but also shorten the energy bandgap and enhanced the separation and transfer of charge at the junction interface, simultaneously, the e- reacted with O2 in the reduction reaction to generate •O2 -. In addition, the recombination rate of e--h+ pairs was further reduced after adding PS to the CNC system due to the reaction between S2O8 2- and e- [52].

In a nutshell, the e- on CB of CNC and CDs, could activate PS to generate •SO4 -, and •SO4 - further reacted with H2O to produce •OH [53]. In this process, the transmission and separation of e--h+ pairs were promoted, and the photocatalytic activity of photogenerated h+ was increased because of the utilization of photogenerated e-. Finally, •OH, h+, •O2 - and •SO4 - achieved the degradation of TC through complex interactions.

The active free radicals could be generated in the CNC/PS system through the following ways:

In the photocatalytic reaction of TC, the intermediate products were analyzed using HPLC-MS, and three possible degradation pathways for TC were proposed. In Fig. 14, the photodegradation process of TC mainly included four steps: ring formation, hydroxylation, ring-opening，and demethylation [24,54,55]. Once dissolved in water, TC was hydrolyzed and protonated (m/z = 445.0). In pathway I, TC was first dehydrated to form P1 (m/z = 427.68). After 10 min of photocatalytic reaction, intermediates P2, P3，and P6 (m/z = 300.14, 240.08，and 150.07) were obtained, in which P6 was formed by opening the ring of P3. In Pathway II, TC was firstly degraded to generate P4 (m/z = 431.14) through N-demethylation reaction, and then P4 was converted to P5 (m/z = 276.10) through ring-opening and hydroxylation reactions. Immediately afterwards, the P6 as the product of P5 was gradually oxidized to low molecule weight intermediates P7, P8，and P9 (m/z = 116.05, 76.02，and 84.01). Finally, these small intermediates were oxidized into CO2 and H2O. In the pathway Ⅲ, TC firstly occurred hydroxylation reaction to produce P11 (m/z = 460.15), and further generated P12 (m/z = 458.13) through the hydrogen abstraction reaction. Next, •OH radical continued to attack P7 to form P13 (m/z = 309.09) and P14 (m/z = 212.84) through dealkylation, dehydroxylation, deamidation，and dehydration reactions.

The mineralization of TC was evaluated by monitoring the decrease of total organic carbon (TOC) during the degradation (Fig. S4). After 60 min of irradiation, about 81.9% of TC was decomposed, while only about 58.8% of the TOC was removed. Combined with the degradation pathway of TC, some more stable products were indeed produced in the photocatalytic process.

The LED-light-driven photocatalyst CNC was successfully prepared by simple thermal polymerization of CDs solution and urea, simultaneously, it was combined with PS to degrade TC. A high degradation rate of 81.9% was obtained in the CNC/PS system, and the optimal operating parameters including CNC dose, PS doses, initial pH value，and TC concentration were determined. It is also demonstrated that the CNC/PS system had good environmental adaptability (pH 2–12), and operational stability (TC removal >80% after five cycles). Moreover, the •O2 - and h+ were regarded as the dominant active radicals for TC degradation, while the reaction between PS and photogenerated e- effectively promoted the separation of e--h+ pairs, thereby enhancing the formation of •O2 - and h+. In addition, possible intermediate products and degradation pathways of TC in CNC/PS were discussed based on HPLC-MS analysis. This work explored an efficient photocatalytic system that combined photocatalysis and AOPs, which had practical application value for environmental governance.
